Yeah, I, I, I can take that one.
I think for us.
As the market has shifted, we're seeing a lot of lease ups stay in the portfolio a little bit longer than, you know, we're used to a couple of years ago when it was the good old days.
And so now we have all of this extra asset management work that we were not anticipating, um, for the lease ups.

So we have several development deals that are Eventually going to go to the market just not as quick as they used to go, right, in the, in the old model in the old world.

But with that cause it's a lot of pricing advisory services that we're having to do manually, um, and we're somewhat feeling comfortable with going to market and understanding what are the options to do this a little bit differently and obviously apartment IQ has been a good partner for us on the market survey side, so we're just taking that exploration step of How can we assist in those?
Um, in those specific situations, I will say that rainwater holistically is not a, um, not, not moving towards a revenue management system.
It's a case by case, need by need basis.

It really is an as needed provider as we evaluate.

when you look at the portfolio today, if you're willing to share, uh, cadence, it sounds like case by case, client by client, uh, I'm certain as well, um.

So I would say Rainwater is very elementary and in the use of the software versus doing it the the good old fashioned way.

number 2 is probably the easability and the ease of use of like, are you making smarter decisions faster than we could in a manual spreadsheet?

Yeah.
